symbols: backward glance — awareness of being watched, the invasion of privacy
garden wall with ivy — the enclosed garden of modesty and virtue
white drapery — purity and innocence under threat
twilight sky — the approaching danger and moral darkness
description: A young woman is depicted seated with her back to the viewer, partially draped in white fabric. Her dark hair falls past her shoulders. She turns her head to look back over her right shoulder with an expression of alert concern or apprehension. The setting is a bathing area beside a stone wall covered in ivy, with a glimpse of twilight sky and trees visible through an opening. The light is warm and intimate, emphasizing the vulnerability of the figure.
theological_description: Susanna’s bath is the opening scene of the story told in the deuterocanonical additions to Daniel (Daniel 13 / Susanna 1-64). Susanna, a beautiful and God-fearing wife, was bathing in her garden when two elders who had been secretly watching her attempted to coerce her into adultery, threatening false accusations if she refused. Susanna chose righteousness over survival: ‘It is better for me to fall into your hands without guilt than to sin in the sight of the Lord’ (Susanna 1:23). Her backward glance captures the moment of dawning awareness that her virtue is under threat. The story becomes a powerful narrative of divine vindication when the young prophet Daniel exposes the elders’ lies through cross-examination (Susanna 1:45-62). Susanna’s story prefigures the theology of unjust accusation and divine defense found throughout Scripture—God is the defender of the innocent (Psalm 37:6) and the one who brings hidden things to light (1 Corinthians 4:5).

scripture_refs.reason: Susanna bathing in her garden and the elders’ attempt to coerce her
Susanna’s cry to God for vindication and God’s answer through Daniel
God bringing forth righteousness as the light and justice as the noonday
